query: Mechanism of action of deferoxamine in iron poisoning

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Deferoxamine (DFO) is a medication used for iron (approved indication) and aluminum toxicity (off-label). It is in the chelator class of drugs. Deferoxamine chelates non-transferrin bound iron (free iron), iron in transit between transferrin and ferritin (labile chelating iron pool), hemosiderin, and ferritin. This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse reactions, interactions, and other factors such as off-label use, dosing, indications, monitoring, and pharmacokinetics. The information is pertinent for members of interprofessional teams in the treatment of iron toxicity and aluminum toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Iron Overload Management
retrieved_docs.content: Iron chelation therapy provides an effective alternative for patients who cannot undergo phlebotomy due to anemia or low hemoglobin levels. Deferoxamine, administered subcutaneously over 8 to 12 hours on multiple days per week, is commonly used in chronic overload. Intravenous administration is reserved for acute toxicity. Adverse effects include hypotension, dizziness, rash, neurotoxicity, and increased risk of Yersinia sepsis or acute respiratory distress syndrome. [46] Oral chelators like deferasirox and deferiprone offer effective management with greater convenience and fewer burdens than deferoxamine. [47] [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: In aluminum toxicity, the appropriate dosing and therapy duration of the drug is uncertain and should be tailored by serum aluminum levels, symptoms, and response. The National Kidney Foundation has listed specific guidelines for the use of deferoxamine in aluminum toxicity, which this article will cover. A proposed mechanism by the Kidney Disease Outcomes Quality Initiative states that in symptomatic patients with serum aluminum levels >60 μg/L but <200 μg/L or a rise in aluminum after DFO >50 μg/L, deferoxamine should be given to treat the aluminum overload. Deferoxamine's ability to draw aluminum out of tissues and into the plasma makes its use very dangerous in those patients with serum aluminum levels >200 μg/L. To avoid DFO-induced neurotoxicity in these patients, its use should delay until the completion of intensive dialysis (6 days per week) with a high-flux dialysis membrane and a dialysate aluminum level of <5 μg/L and until the pre-dialysis serum aluminum level has been reduced to <200 μg/L.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Disposition
retrieved_docs.content: Hospital admission is recommended for patients with persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, clinical dehydration, or the need for deferoxamine therapy. Intensive care unit admission is indicated for individuals presenting with shock, coma, metabolic acidosis, or serum iron concentrations exceeding 1000 mcg/dL. Psychiatric evaluation is essential in all cases of intentional overdose.

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Deferoxamine (DFO or DFOA) is FDA approved to treat iron overload, either acute or chronic. The definition of iron overload is serial ferritin levels above 800 to 3000 ng/mL. [1] The FDA has not approved deferoxamine as first-line therapy for hereditary hemochromatosis unless there is a contraindication to phlebotomy. Clinicians can also use deferoxamine as an off-label treatment for aluminum toxicity in chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Deferoxamine can also bind aluminum within the plasma to form aluminoxane, which is renally excreted. In the case of CKD/ESRD patients, the product is dialyzable using a high-flux membrane. [20] Deferoxamine can draw aluminum deposited in tissues into the plasma. [20] For this reason, patients with a measured serum aluminum concentration >200 mcg/L should not be treated with deferoxamine as it may lead to severely high levels of aluminum and fatal neurotoxicity. The recommended administration will be discussed in the following section.

retrieved_docs.title: Antidotes -- Clinical Significance -- Treatment and Antidotes
retrieved_docs.content: Iron : For severe iron overdose IV deferoxamine is the treatment of choice. Deferoxamine acts as a chelating agent that binds with ferric iron forming ferrioxamine which is water-soluble and excreted by the kidneys.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Acute Iron Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic toxicity, persistent gastrointestinal symptoms, or hemodynamic instability require more intensive treatment, often including admission to an intensive care unit. Key interventions include intravenous crystalloid resuscitation to correct hypovolemia and support organ perfusion. Chelation with deferoxamine is indicated for patients with serum iron concentrations greater than 500 mcg/dL, metabolic acidosis, shock, or evidence of organ dysfunction. Deferoxamine is administered as a continuous intravenous infusion at 15 mg/kg/hour, with a maximum of 35 mg/kg/hour. The maximum daily dose is 6 g. Therapy normally continues for 24 hours, depending on the clinical course. Toxicology consultation is strongly recommended. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Siderosis Bulbi -- Treatment / Management -- Role of Deferoxamine
retrieved_docs.content: Deferoxamine is a chelating agent with a high affinity for free iron ions. It is used in thalassaemic patients undergoing multiple blood transfusions to avoid siderosis due to iron overload. [67] Subconjunctival use of deferoxamine (10 to 100 mg) has been shown to prevent the development of ocular siderosis. In advanced siderosis, where cells are already damaged, deferoxamine cannot reverse the toxic effects of iron as it cannot remove the bound iron ions from the tissue. [68] Deferoxamine-related toxicity, such as bone dysplasia, sensorineural hearing loss, nyctalopia, color vision impairment, RPE alteration, and reduction in ERG amplitudes, limits its use in the management of ocular siderosis. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Deferoxamine, a chelating agent that can remove iron from tissues and free iron from plasma, is indicated in patients with systemic toxicity, metabolic acidosis, worsening symptoms, or a serum iron level predictive of moderate or severe toxicity. It is administered as a continuous infusion at 15 mg/kg/hr for up to 24 hours with a maximum dose of 35 mg/kg/hr if there is no rate-related hypotension. The maximum daily dose is 6 g. Clinical recovery guides the termination of deferoxamine therapy but the duration of therapy is typically 24 hours. Consultation with a toxicologist is highly recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of deferoxamine. Describe the potential adverse effects of deferoxamine. Review the appropriate monitoring for patients using deferoxamine. Outline interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the use of deferoxamine and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Overload and Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Management of Acute Iron Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Whole-bowel irrigation using polyethylene glycol via nasogastric tube, at a rate of 1.5 to 2 L/hour in adults, facilitates the removal of unabsorbed tablets. Gastric lavage may be considered when imaging reveals large numbers of retained tablets and is performed early, although potential risks often outweigh benefits. Coagulopathy should be addressed with vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma. Activated charcoal does not bind iron and should not be used.

retrieved_docs.title: Iron Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Activated charcoal binds iron poorly and is not effective.

retrieved_docs.title: Deferoxamine -- Toxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Patients tolerate deferoxamine well, and there is no specific antidote for the medication. The precautions and dose reductions are described elsewhere in this paper.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Iron Overload -- Complications -- Deferoxamine
retrieved_docs.content: Retinal damage causing night blindness, visual field loss, and retinal pigmentation High-tone sensorineural hearing loss Growth and bone defects in children causing rickets-like bone lesions, metaphyseal changes, and spinal damage Calculate a therapeutic index as the mean daily dose (mg/kg)/current serum ferritin (μg/L). If this is < 0.025 at all times, these side effects of DFO do not occur.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Iron Overload -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Commonly used chelators in the United States are deferoxamine (DFO), deferiprone, and deferasirox. Before initiating or altering iron chelation therapy, assess patients' iron loading and previous chelation rates.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Iron chelators are a class of agents that are linked to iron, with iron serving as the parent entity for these chelating compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2003
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations are classified as members of the sodium ironedetate group, indicating that sodium ironedetate is a specific type of iron trivalent oral antianemic medication used to treat iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations represent a therapeutic class of iron‑based agents used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, and this class includes specific compounds such as ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate. Both ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate are members of the iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations group, providing clinicians with oral options to replenish deficient iron stores in patients. Understanding that these individual agents belong to the same antianemic class helps guide appropriate selection and dosing in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: FERROUS AMINO ACID CHELATE is a pharmaceutical preparation that combines iron with folic acid and is classified as a member of the iron‑in‑combination‑with‑folic‑acid group, reflecting its role in providing both elemental iron and folate for the treatment of iron deficiency and support of folate-dependent metabolic processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 382
retrieved_docs.content: provides multiple iron salts (ferrous fumarate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous cysteine glycinate) together with vitamin C derivatives (ascorbic acid, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) that facilitate iron uptake, and a spectrum of B‑vitamins and cofactors that support erythropoiesis and overall metabolic health. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how specific oral iron preparations—whether bivalent iron‑ascorbate salts or broader multivitamin‑iron combinations—are organized within therapeutic classes and formulated to maximize iron bioavailability while addressing concurrent micronutrient deficiencies commonly seen in patients with iron deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 402
retrieved_docs.content: 1 mg, BETAINE 500 µg, CITRIC ACID MONOHYDRATE 1.83 mg, SODIUM CITRATE 3.67 mg, as well as ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, niacin 3 mg, and polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg. This extensive composition provides folate, iron, vitamin C, B‑vitamins, magnesium, zinc, and other cofactors that support hematologic health, antioxidant defenses, and metabolic processes. Gentamicin‑sulfát is noted to have a relationship with manganese, indicating a potential pharmacologic interaction or consideration when these agents are co‑administered. Clinicians should be aware of this link to avoid possible adverse effects or altered efficacy of the aminoglycoside antibiotic when patients are also receiving manganese‑containing supplements or formulations. In summary, manganese serves as a common active component in both a multi‑mineral oral liquid and a multivitamin/iron capsule, the latter delivering a comprehensive array of vitamins and minerals. The identified connection between

retrieved_docs.content: (FEFM‑FEPOL) 106 MG MULTIVITAMINS CAP is a delayed‑release capsule containing a complex of leucovorin, folic acid, levomefolate magnesium, ferrous cysteine glycinate, specialized phospholipids (1,2‑docosahexanoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium, 1,2‑icosapentoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium), phosphatidyl‑serine, pyridoxal phosphate, flavin adenine dinucleotide, NADH, cobamamide, co‑carboxylase, magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate, magnesium L‑threonate, betaine, citric acid monohydrate, sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, ferrous fumarate, niacin, and polysaccharide iron complex. The FA 1 MG/IRON capsule is listed as an ingredient of a preparation called “mangan,” which also includes gentamicin‑sulfát. retrieved_docs.content: therapies, while ferric maltol, ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate serve dual roles as nutritional iron supplements and, in some cases, industrial aids. This integrated view aids clinicians in understanding the multifaceted uses of these agents across therapeutic, nutritional and industrial domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1349
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol is classified as a hematinic, indicating that it belongs to the group of agents used to increase hemoglobin levels and treat iron‑deficiency anemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: capsules, and tigecycline is provided as a 50 mg injection. The formulations illustrate extensive variability in dosage strength, release profile (immediate, delayed, or extended‑release), route of administration (oral or injectable), and excipient composition (iron oxide, alginic acid), all of which clinicians must consider when selecting antimicrobial therapy or when managing patients with excipient sensitivities. Additionally, the cholinesterase inhibitor galantamine hydrobromide shares the same iron‑oxide inactive ingredient, underscoring the importance of recognizing common excipients across therapeutic classes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations are classified as members of the askorbát železnatý (iron‑ascorbate) group, indicating that these oral anemia treatments contain bivalent iron complexed with ascorbic acid.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 50
retrieved_docs.content: leucovorin 2.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, levomefolate magnesium 7 mg, ferrous cysteine glycinate 13.6 mg, specialized phospholipids (1,2‑docosahexaenoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium 6.4 mg and 1,2‑icosapentoyl‑SN‑glycero‑3‑phosphoserine calcium 800 µg), phosphatidyl‑serine 12 mg, pyridoxal‑5′‑phosphate 25 µg, flavin adenine dinucleotide 25 µg, NADH 25 µg, cobamamide 50 µg, cocarboxylase 25 µg, magnesium ascorbate 24 mg, zinc ascorbate 1 mg, magnesium L‑threonate 1 mg, betaine 500 µg, citric‑acid monohydrate 1.83 mg, sodium citrate 3.67 mg, and other delayed‑release excipients. A second version of the same blend contains ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, niacin 3 mg and a polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg. The therapeutic rationale for pairing folic acid with iron is their synergistic support of erythropoiesis: folic acid is essential for DNA synthesis and red‑cell maturation, and its deficiency causes macrocytic anemia; iron is required for hemoglobin

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 953
retrieved_docs.content: synthesis. Folic acid, levomefolate magnesium, and cobamamide (a form of vitamin B12) address folate and cobalamin deficiencies that can also cause anemia. Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and its salts (magnesium ascorbate, zinc ascorbate) enhance intestinal iron absorption, while additional vitamins (pyridoxal phosphate, niacin, flavin adenine dinucleotide) and minerals (magnesium, zinc, calcium) support overall metabolic processes involved in erythropoiesis. This integrated multivitamin‑iron formulation therefore provides a comprehensive therapeutic approach for the prevention and treatment of various forms of anemia by simultaneously correcting iron deficiency, folate deficiency, vitamin B12 deficiency, and supporting co‑factor availability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862
retrieved_docs.content: of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.
